title: AI Fundamentals
description: Core AI, ML, and GenAI concepts every candidate should be able to explain clearly.

questions.title: Explain the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI.
questions.scenario: A business stakeholder asks whether every AI roadmap item should use an LLM.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer separates the broad field of AI from machine learning systems that learn patterns from data, deep learning models based on neural networks, and Generative AI systems that create text, code, images, or structured outputs. The candidate should also explain that not every AI feature needs an LLM; classification, forecasting, anomaly detection, and ranking may be better solved with traditional ML. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A business stakeholder asks whether every AI roadmap item should use an LLM. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by mapping the concept to a concrete workflow, choosing the simplest suitable technique, validating it with data or user feedback, and explaining how it changes the product or platform behavior.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Explain the difference between AI, ML, deep learning, and Generative AI.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real ai fundamentals setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: Compare supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning.
questions.scenario: You need to select an approach for fraud labels, customer segmentation, and an agent optimizing a workflow.
questions.expectedAnswer: Supervised learning uses labeled examples to learn input-output mappings. Unsupervised learning discovers patterns without labels, such as clusters or lower-dimensional structure. Reinforcement learning trains an agent to choose actions that maximize reward through interaction with an environment. A good answer includes where each approach fits and why data availability drives the choice. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: You need to select an approach for fraud labels, customer segmentation, and an agent optimizing a workflow. questions.title: What is the difference between training and inference?
questions.scenario: Your team trained a model offline and now needs to expose it through an API.
questions.expectedAnswer: Training is the offline or iterative process of learning model parameters from data. Inference is using the trained model to make predictions or generate outputs for new inputs. Production inference emphasizes latency, throughput, cost, reliability, monitoring, and safety, while training emphasizes data quality, optimization, validation, and experimentation. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: How do you explain overfitting and underfitting?
questions.scenario: A model has excellent training accuracy but poor validation accuracy.
questions.expectedAnswer: Overfitting happens when a model learns noise or overly specific training patterns and fails to generalize. Underfitting happens when the model is too simple, poorly trained, or missing important features. Candidates should discuss train-validation gaps, regularization, validation design, leakage detection, and remedies such as more data, better features, early stopping, or simpler models. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: How do you decide whether to build or buy an AI solution?
questions.scenario: Leadership asks whether to buy a vendor AI assistant or build an internal enterprise assistant.
questions.expectedAnswer: The decision depends on strategic differentiation, data sensitivity, integration complexity, time to market, cost, compliance, quality requirements, vendor lock-in, and internal operating maturity. Buy when the use case is commodity and speed matters. Build when the data, workflow, model behavior, or governance requirements create durable differentiation. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.
